2: anochi YHWH eloheicha asher hotzeiticha meeretz mitzraim mibeit avadim: 'I am YHWH your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of slavery.' The Decalogue opens not with a command but with a redemption narrative — the commands follow from the prior act of grace. YHWH does not say 'obey me so that I will be your God and bring you out' but 'I brought you out; therefore I am your God; therefore obey.' The evangelical-imperative structure of the Decalogue is: grace, then obligation. Paul's ethical sections follow the same pattern: Romans 1-11 (gospel), then 12-16 (imperatives); Galatians 1-4 (grace), then 5-6 (walk).
1: vayedaber Elohim et kol-hadevarim ha'eleh lemor (waydabbēr ʾĕlōhîm ʾēt kol-haddĕbārîm hāʾēlleh lēʾmōr): 'God spoke all these words, saying.' The direct divine speech of the Decalogue is unique in the OT — YHWH speaking directly to the entire assembled people, not through a prophet. Kol-hadevarim ha'eleh (all these words): the Decalogue is presented as divine speech, not human legislation. The authority of the Ten Commandments derives entirely from their speaker, not from their reasonableness or social utility.
3: lo yihyeh lecha elohim acherim al panai (lōʾ yihyeh-lĕkā ʾĕlōhîm ʾăḥērîm ʿal-pānāy): 'You shall have no other gods before my face.' Al panai (before my face/in my presence): the commandment is spatially framed — in YHWH's presence there is no room for other allegiances. The command is not primarily a philosophical monotheism but a covenant loyalty: YHWH's face defines the space of relationship, and in that space the exclusive claim holds. Elohim acherim (other gods): the commandment does not deny the existence of other supernatural powers but prohibits their veneration in YHWH's presence.
4: lo ta'aseh lecha pesel vechol-temunah asher bashamayim mima'al (lōʾ taʿăśeh-lĕkā pesel wĕḵol-tĕmûnāh ʾăšer baššāmāyim mimmaʿal): 'You shall not make for yourself an image/idol or any likeness of what is in heaven above.' Pesel (H6459, idol/image): from pasal (to cut/chisel) — a carved representation. The prohibition covers all representations of the divine in material form: sky, earth, or sea. The rationale emerges from the Sinai theophany: Israel heard a voice but saw no form (Deut 4:12 — you heard a voice but saw no form). YHWH's invisibility is theologically determinative: to make an image is to assign a form to what has no form, to domesticate what is beyond representation.
5: lo tishtachaveh lahem velo ta'avdem ki anochi YHWH Eloheicha El qanna (lōʾ-ṯiŝtaḥăweh lāhem wĕlōʾ ṯoʿoḇdēm kî ʾānōkî Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā ʾēl qannāʾ): 'You shall not bow down to them or serve them, for I, YHWH your God, am a jealous God.' El qanna (H7072, jealous God): qanna from qana (H7065, be jealous/zealous) — used exclusively of YHWH in the OT (Exod 34:14; Deut 4:24; 5:9; 6:15; Josh 24:19; Nah 1:2). Divine jealousy is not pettiness but the passion of a covenant Lord who has committed himself exclusively to his people and therefore cannot tolerate their divided loyalty. The jealousy of YHWH corresponds to the covenant's exclusive demand: monogamy and monotheism have the same structure. Poked avon avot al-banim: visiting the iniquity of the fathers on the children — the generational transmission of covenant violation (not arbitrary punishment but the natural consequence of covenantal patterns passing down).
6: ve'oseh chesed la'alafim le'ohavai uleshomrei mitzvotai: 'Showing steadfast love to thousands of those who love me and keep my commandments.' The asymmetry: iniquity to the third and fourth generation; chesed (H2617, steadfast love/covenant faithfulness) to alafim (thousands of generations). The divine mercy vastly exceeds the divine judgment in scope. The judgment extends three or four generations; the chesed extends to thousands. YHWH's primary orientation is mercy, not wrath; judgment is the reluctant instrument, love is the determinative character.
7: lo tissa et-shem YHWH Eloheicha lashav (lōʾ tiśśāʾ ʾet-šēm Yhwh ʾĕlōhêkā laššāwĕʾ): 'You shall not lift/bear the name of YHWH your God for emptiness/vanity.' Tissa (H5375, lift/carry/bear): the verb is not about swearing but bearing — the name of YHWH is something carried. Shav (H7723, emptiness/vanity/worthlessness): not merely false oaths but any use of the divine name for trivial, empty, or purposeless ends. To bear the divine name (as Israel does as a covenant people) for emptiness is to misuse the covenant identity — whether in false oaths, magic, manipulation, or casual irreverence. The commandment protects the weight of the divine name.
8: zachor et-yom haShabbat lekadsho (zāḵôr ʾet-yôm haššabbāt lĕqaddĕšô): 'Remember the Sabbath day to keep it holy.' Zachor (H2142, remember): the covenantal memory-word — remember is not a passive mental recollection but an active bringing-into-the-present (as in YHWH remembering the covenant in Exod 2:24). Lekadsho (to sanctify/set it apart): the Sabbath's holiness is not intrinsic to the day but given by covenantal act — the same sanctification word used for Sinai (v.8-10). Time is made holy by separation and divine assignment, just as space is made holy.
9: sheshet yamim ta'avod ve'asita kol-melachtecha: 'Six days you shall labor and do all your work.' The positive command precedes the prohibition: six days of productive work are commanded before the seventh of rest. The Sabbath is not the negation of labor but its covenantal framing: human creativity and productivity are honored in six days; the seventh day declares that human work is embedded in a larger divine rhythm and does not define existence. Work receives its meaning from the rest that frames it.
10: veyom hashvii Shabbat laYHWH Eloheicha lo ta'aseh khol-melacha atah uvinkha uvitecha avdecha va'amatcha uvhemtecha: 'But the seventh day is a Sabbath to YHWH your God. You shall not do any work, you or your son or your daughter, your servant or your maidservant, your livestock, or the stranger within your gates.' The comprehensive scope of the Sabbath rest extends to every level of the social hierarchy: children, servants, animals, foreigners — the Sabbath creates a covenantal equality of rest. Even the animals and the alien resident participate in the covenant's rhythm of rest. No social hierarchy survives the Sabbath day.
11: ki sheshet yamim asah YHWH et-hashamayim ve'et-ha'aretz vayinafash (kî šēšet-yāmîm ʿāśāh Yhwh ʾet-haššāmayim wĕʾet-hāʾāreṣ wayyinnāpaš): 'For in six days YHWH made the heavens and earth, and rested (nefesh) on the seventh day.' Vayinafash (H5314): from nefesh (soul/breath/life) — YHWH breathed/refreshed/took breath. The anthropomorphic language is striking: YHWH's rest on the seventh day is described as the refreshment of a living being. Human Sabbath rest participates in the divine rest-pattern embedded in creation — the weekly rhythm is cosmologically grounded, not merely socially convenient.
12: kabed et-avicha ve'et-imecha (kabbēd ʾet-ʾābîkā wĕʾet-ʾimmekā): 'Honor your father and your mother.' Kabed (H3513, honor/be heavy): from the root meaning heavy/weighty — to honor is to give weight to, to treat as consequential. The commandment grounds social order in the family: the first four commandments address the vertical relationship (Israel to YHWH); this commandment transitions to the horizontal (Israelite to Israelite) by beginning with the foundational human relationship. The promise attached — that your days may be long in the land — ties individual obedience to national covenantal blessing.
13: lo tirtzach (lōʾ tirṣāḥ): 'You shall not murder.' Ratzach (H7523, murder): specifically premeditated, willful killing — not the same as harag (H2026, kill generally) or hemit (H4191, put to death). The commandment prohibits intentional, unauthorized taking of human life — a prohibition grounded in the image of God (Gen 9:6: whoever sheds the blood of man, by man shall his blood be shed, for God made man in his own image). Human life is inviolable because the image of God makes each person a representative of the Creator.
14: lo tin'af (lōʾ tinʾāp): 'You shall not commit adultery.' Na'af (H5003, commit adultery): the sexual corollary of the first commandment's covenant-loyalty requirement. Marriage is the human covenant that structurally mirrors the divine covenant; adultery is the human betrayal that structurally mirrors idolatry. The prophets consistently use adultery as the metaphor for Israel's covenant-breaking (Jer 3:8; Ezek 16; Hos 1-3). The prohibition protects both the marriage covenant and the community's covenantal integrity.
15: lo tignov (lōʾ tiganob): 'You shall not steal.' Ganav (H1589, steal): the taking of what belongs to another — whether property, persons (kidnapping is also covered under this root, Exod 21:16), or anything belonging to another. Respect for the neighbor's possessions is grounded in respect for the neighbor as a fellow covenant member with God-given rights. The command extends the image-of-God protection from life (v.13) to dignity (v.14) to property (v.15).
16: lo ta'aneh vere'acha ed shav (lōʾ-taʿăneh bĕrēʿăkā ʿēd šāwĕʾ): 'You shall not testify against your neighbor as a false witness.' Ed shav (witness of emptiness/vanity): shav is the same word as in v.7 — the emptiness that misuses the divine name is the same emptiness that perverts testimony. The legal system depends on truthful testimony; false witness destroys the possibility of justice. The commandment protects the covenantal community's capacity to adjudicate rightly — the court as the arena of covenant justice.
17: lo tachmod beit re'echa lo tachmod eshet re'echa ve'avdo va'amato veshoro vachamoro (lōʾ taḥmōd bêt rēʿăkā lōʾ taḥmōd ʾēšet rēʿăkā wĕʿaḇdô wăʾămāṯô wĕšôrô waḥămōrô): 'You shall not covet your neighbor's house. You shall not covet your neighbor's wife or his servant or his maidservant or his ox or his donkey or anything that belongs to your neighbor.' Chamad (H2530, covet/desire intensely): the desire for what belongs to another, named for the attractive pull it exerts. Unique among the commandments in targeting inner desire rather than external action — the only commandment that can only be violated before the act. Paul cites this commandment as the one that awakened his awareness of sin (Rom 7:7: I would not have known covetousness if the law had not said, you shall not covet).
18: vekhol-ha'am ro'im et-haqolot ve'et-halappidim ve'et qol hashofar ve'et-hahar ashen vayar ha'am vayanu'u vaya'amdu merachok: 'All the people saw the thunderings and the flashes and the sound of the trumpet and the mountain smoking. When the people saw it, they trembled and stood far off.' The synesthetic description — seeing thunder and lightning, the visible and audible blended — is the phenomenology of theophany. The people's trembling response and their standing far off is the appropriate creaturely response to unconditioned divine presence.
19: vayomeru el-Mosheh daber-atah imanu venishma'ah ve'al-yedaber imanu Elohim pen-namut: 'They said to Moses: You speak to us and we will listen, but let not God speak to us lest we die.' The people's request for a human mediator is not disobedience but honest self-knowledge: they cannot sustain the direct encounter with YHWH. Their fear validates the necessity of the Mosaic mediatorial office. The pattern repeats: Israel needs a mediator between YHWH's holiness and their creaturely limitation. Moses is the type; Christ is the antitype — the one mediator between God and humanity (1 Tim 2:5) who resolves the mediatorial need permanently.
20: vayomer Mosheh el-ha'am al-tira'u ki livliti nassot etchem ba ha'Elohim uvavur tihyeh yirato al-peneichem: 'Moses said to the people: Do not be afraid, for God has come to test you, that the fear of him may be before you, that you may not sin.' The purpose of the theophanic terror: livliti nassot etchem (to test you) and yirato al-peneichem (his fear before your face). The terrifying encounter is not punishment but pedagogy — the fear generated is the appropriate orientation toward YHWH that prevents sin. The theology of fear and trembling (Phil 2:12 — work out your salvation with fear and trembling) is rooted in this Sinai principle.
21: vaya'amod ha'am merachok veMosheh nigash el-ha'arafel: 'The people stood far off, and Moses drew near to the thick darkness where God was.' Arafel (H6205, thick darkness/deep darkness): the darkness that surrounds the divine light — not the absence of YHWH but his concealment within an overwhelming cloud. The people stand far off; Moses enters the darkness alone. The covenant mediator goes where the people cannot follow — into the thick of divine presence. Hebrews uses this pattern: Christ entered not a human sanctuary but heaven itself (Heb 9:24), the true most holy place where we cannot go except through him.
22: vayomer YHWH el-Mosheh ko tomar el-bnei Yisrael atem re'item ki min-hashamayim dibarti imachem: 'YHWH said to Moses: Thus you shall say to the children of Israel: You yourselves have seen that I have spoken with you from heaven.' The direct appeal to witnessed experience: atem re'item (you yourselves have seen). The covenant is grounded not in tradition alone but in the community's own witnessed encounter with the divine voice. The appeal to testimony-based faith is the OT foundation for the NT's eyewitness theology (1 John 1:1-3: what we have seen and heard we proclaim to you).
23: lo ta'asun iti elohei kesef ve'elohei zahav lo ta'asun lachem: 'You shall not make alongside me gods of silver and of gold; you shall not make such for yourselves.' The prohibition immediately follows the theophany — the community that has just heard YHWH's voice directly is immediately warned against the idol-substitute. The golden calf (Exod 32) will violate this commandment within weeks. The speed of Israel's violation underscores the commandment's necessity: human inclination toward tangible gods is powerful and persistent.
24: mizbach adamah ta'aseh li vezavachta alav et-oloteicha ve'et-shlameicha et-tsoncha ve'et-beqarecha: 'An altar of earth you shall make for me and sacrifice on it your burnt offerings and peace offerings, your sheep and your oxen.' The simple altar permitted after the Decalogue — earth or unhewn stone (v.25) — sets the minimalist principle for early Israelite worship: the altar's simplicity declares YHWH's sufficiency. No human craftsmanship adorns the first altar; the only material is what the earth already provides. The elaborate tabernacle and temple are later, more specific instructions; this is the baseline.
25: ve'im-mizbach avanim ta'aseh-li lo-tivneh ethen gazit ki charbecha haneifta aleiha vatechaleleiha: 'And if you make me a stone altar, you shall not build it of cut stones, for if you wield your tool on it you profane it.' The prohibition of dressed stone for the altar: human tool-work profanes the altar — the natural, uncut stone preserves its integrity. The rationale is contested (the stones' natural completeness, or the avoidance of phallic symbols carved on stones). The principle: human craftsmanship is not required to make the altar acceptable to YHWH; what YHWH provides is sufficient for approach to him.
26: velo ta'aleh bema'alot al-mizbechi asher lo-tigaleh ervatecha alav: 'You shall not go up by steps to my altar so that your nakedness is not uncovered on it.' The altar's access must not involve raised stairs that expose the priest's nakedness — a combination of modesty requirement and anti-Canaanite contrast (Canaanite altar worship involved fertility rites). The practical regulation shapes the covenantal aesthetics of worship: the holy space requires an approach that maintains the dignity and integrity of those who serve in it.
